title: Antimicrobial Treatment in Category 2 & 3 Restoration — EPA-Registered Products, Application Rates & Dwell Time
description: A technical guide to antimicrobial treatment in Australian water damage restoration — selecting EPA-registered products for Category 2 and 3 jobs, correct application rates, required dwell times, and documentation for insurance claims. Covers IICRC S500:2025 §7.6.
author: Phill McGurk | RestoreAssist
transcript:
## When Is Antimicrobial Treatment Required?

Antimicrobial treatment in water damage restoration refers to the application of EPA-registered products to building materials and surfaces to inhibit microbial growth. Under IICRC S500:2025 §7.6, antimicrobial treatment is required on all Category 2 and Category 3 jobs where porous or semi-porous materials have been affected.

It is important to understand that antimicrobial treatment does not substitute for physical removal of contaminated materials. In Category 3 scenarios, porous materials must be removed regardless of whether they have been treated. Treatment is an adjunct to — not a replacement for — appropriate removal and cleaning.

## Australian Regulatory Framework

In Australia, antimicrobial products used in restoration are regulated by the Australian Pesticides and Veterinary Medicines Authority (APVMA) under the Agricultural and Veterinary Chemicals Code Act. Products must be registered with APVMA for the specific use claimed — a product registered as a household disinfectant may not be legally used as a biocide for mould remediation unless that use is on the product label.

Always check the APVMA product registration before using any antimicrobial product on a restoration job. Using an unregistered product, or a product outside its registered use pattern, creates legal liability and potentially invalidates the treatment for insurance purposes.

## Common Antimicrobial Product Categories

### Quaternary Ammonium Compounds (Quats)
Quats are the most widely used category of antimicrobial in restoration. Products such as benzalkonium chloride are effective against a broad spectrum of bacteria and some fungi. They have residual activity (continued action after the surface dries), are relatively low-toxicity, and are compatible with most building materials.

**Limitation**: Quats can be inactivated by organic soils — surfaces must be physically cleaned before application for effective antimicrobial action.

### Oxidising Agents (Hydrogen Peroxide, Chlorine Dioxide)
Oxidising agents are broad-spectrum and effective against bacteria, fungi, and viruses. Hydrogen peroxide formulations are commonly used in Australian restoration for Category 3 decontamination. They break down to water and oxygen, leaving no residue — an advantage in food-handling areas or when reoccupation is imminent.

**Limitation**: Limited residual activity and may bleach some surfaces at higher concentrations. Handle with appropriate PPE.

### Enzyme-Based Products
Enzymatic products break down biological soils (proteins, fats) and are particularly effective as a first step before disinfectant application on Category 3 losses or protein fire residue. They are not disinfectants and do not have antimicrobial registration for use alone — they are used in conjunction with a registered disinfectant.

## Application Requirements

For any antimicrobial treatment to be effective, three requirements must be met:

1. **Surface preparation**: The surface must be visibly clean. Antimicrobials applied over organic soil, residue, or heavily contaminated surfaces will be partially or fully inactivated before they can act on the microorganisms below.

2. **Correct concentration**: Products must be applied at the concentration specified on the label for the intended use. Diluting below the specified rate reduces efficacy; exceeding it may damage materials and creates unnecessary chemical exposure.

3. **Required dwell time**: The product label specifies the contact time (dwell time) required for the claimed efficacy — typically 5–10 minutes for broad-spectrum disinfection. The surface must remain wet for this period. Applying the product and immediately wiping or rinsing it off does not achieve the claimed antimicrobial effect.

## Documenting Antimicrobial Treatment

Insurance documentation for antimicrobial treatment should record:
- Product name and APVMA registration number
- Application concentration and dilution ratio used
- Surfaces and areas treated (referenced to the floor plan)
- Application method (spray, wipe, fog)
- Dwell time allowed
- Date and time of application
- Technician name
videoScript:
G'day, welcome to RestoreAssist. Today we're covering antimicrobial treatment — when it's required, how to do it correctly, and how to document it for the insurance claim.

Under IICRC S500:2025 section 7.6, antimicrobial treatment is required on all Category 2 and Category 3 jobs where porous or semi-porous materials have been affected. But here's the critical point — and I can't stress this enough — antimicrobial treatment does not substitute for physical removal of contaminated materials. If you're on a Category 3 job, the porous materials come out regardless of whether you treat them. Treatment is an additional step, not a shortcut around removal.

In Australia, the products you use are regulated by the APVMA — the Australian Pesticides and Veterinary Medicines Authority. Every antimicrobial product must be APVMA-registered for the specific use you're applying it to. A household disinfectant is not necessarily registered as a restoration biocide. Check the label before you use any product on a job. Using an unregistered product creates legal liability and can invalidate the treatment for insurance purposes.

The three most common product categories in Australian restoration are quats — quaternary ammonium compounds — oxidising agents like hydrogen peroxide, and enzyme-based products.

Quats are the workhorse: broad-spectrum, residual activity, compatible with most surfaces. The critical limitation is they're inactivated by organic soil. The surface must be physically clean before you apply them or the treatment won't work.

Hydrogen peroxide formulations are excellent for Category 3 decontamination — broad-spectrum, break down to water and oxygen, no residue. Limited residual activity, but good for spaces where reoccupation is imminent.

Enzyme-based products break down biological soils and are useful as a pre-treatment before your disinfectant. They are not disinfectants on their own.

For any antimicrobial to work, three things must happen. First, the surface must be visibly clean — organic soil inactivates the product. Second, the product must be applied at the label-specified concentration — not diluted further. Third, the surface must stay wet for the full dwell time specified on the label — typically 5 to 10 minutes. Apply it and immediately wipe it off, and you haven't done anything useful.

For documentation: record the product name, APVMA registration number, concentration, treated surfaces referenced to the floor plan, application method, dwell time, and the date. That's what goes in the insurance claim.

RestoreAssist includes antimicrobial treatment documentation fields in the inspection scope workflow.

Head to restoreassist.app.
